patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 38-year-old woman, anxious and a bit vague. Start with: “I’ve been exhausted ever since I had my baby, and it’s not getting better.” If asked to describe it, volunteer: months of extreme fatigue even after sleep, lightheaded on standing/near-fainting, feeling colder than others, low appetite with unintentional weight loss, and “not myself since delivery.” Mention you drink lots of coffee/energy drinks and sometimes your heart races.
Only if directly asked, disclose: low breast milk supply after birth, no periods since delivery, much lower sex drive, intermittent nausea/“off” stomach, on-and-off headaches since delivery, dry skin and thinning hair.
Do not volunteer unless specifically asked: minimal sweating, thinning underarm/pubic hair, brain fog, constipation, weakness with stairs/carrying baby.
Be resistant/embarrassed about vaginal dryness and painful sex; admit it only with gentle, specific questions. Say you worry it’s just postpartum depression/burnout or anemia from heavy delivery bleeding.
If the clinician shows empathy, soften, become more open, and ask about affordable options. Express cost worries, skipping doses to stretch meds, avoiding labs/visits, and belief that generics are weaker.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose Sheehan syndrome and start correct-sequence hormone replacement with core safety checks in a stable patient.
must_collect: history of postpartum hemorrhage or hypotension
amenorrhea/oligomenorrhea or loss of secondary sexual function
symptoms suggestive of adrenal insufficiency (weight loss, nausea, dizziness)
symptoms suggestive of hypothyroidism (cold intolerance, constipation, weight gain)
contraindications/risk factors for steroids and levothyroxine (e.g., uncontrolled infection, severe cardiac disease)
steps: Communicate diagnosis: Sheehan syndrome due to pituitary injury after childbirth, leading to low pituitary hormones and downstream low cortisol/thyroid hormones.

Safety checks before prescribing: allergy check and contraindication check; review medication list for interactions (e.g., levothyroxine absorption interactions; steroids with NSAIDs/anticoagulants as applicable).
Prescribe hydrocortisone replacement first; provide sick-day rules and emergency escalation instructions.
After hydrocortisone is started, prescribe levothyroxine; counsel on administration (empty stomach, separation from iron/calcium) and monitoring using free T4.

Provide follow-up/monitoring plan verbally (even if not using a follow-up WRITE tool): reassess symptoms, vitals, electrolytes/glucose as appropriate; endocrine follow-up for full pituitary axis evaluation.

acceptable_variants.description: Inpatient/urgent-start variant when patient appears unstable or high risk for adrenal crisis; still records diagnosis and initiates steroid-first therapy, with explicit escalation.
steps: Identify suspected Sheehan syndrome and communicate pituitary-related hormone failure.

Start/plan levothyroxine only after glucocorticoid coverage is ensured and patient is stabilized; titrate by free T4.

acceptable_variants.description: Dose-individualization variant emphasizing comorbidities: lower levothyroxine starting dose in older patients/cardiac disease and clear monitoring targets for central hypothyroidism.
steps: Explain Sheehan syndrome and pituitary failure.

Start hydrocortisone replacement first with education on stress dosing.
Start levothyroxine at a conservative dose if cardiac risk or older age, with plan to titrate to free T4 in upper half of normal; avoid using TSH for titration.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, an anxious but clear-speaking parent bringing your 2-year-old child. Start with: “My baby keeps losing skills and seems weaker every week.” Early in the visit, volunteer: he used to roll/reach but now doesn’t; feels floppy; poor head control; startles easily to loud sounds; episodes like seizures (stiffening/jerking ~1 minute, then very sleepy). If asked about feeding/growth/behavior/vision/breathing/abdomen, share: slower feeds with choking/coughing, poor weight gain, sleepier/less interactive, worse eye tracking, fast breathing after feeds/crying, bigger belly. Do not mention unless directly asked or trust is built: less response to name, constipation, sweating/tiring during feeds, intermittent limb stiffness, more chest colds/cough. Resist sharing at first: brief pauses in breathing during sleep and that you and your partner are cousins; disclose only if the clinician is nonjudgmental and explains why it matters. Offer misconceptions: morning puffiness might be allergy; brief bluish lips when crying could be “calcium/glands”; big belly might be “fat breakdown.” Worry insurance will deny genetic tests/specialists; think palliative/hospice means giving up; assume home nursing is only for wealthy. If met with empathy, soften, share more details, and ask what can be done.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis explicitly using the term "Sandhoff disease" and frames it as a genetic lysosomal storage disorder (GM2 gangliosidosis) with neurodegenerative progression.
States that confirmation and/or family counseling involves genetic testing (e.g., HEXB gene testing) and/or enzyme testing, and offers referral for genetic counseling.
Explains treatment as supportive care, including seizure control, feeding/nutrition support, respiratory care, mobility/physical therapy, and palliative goals as appropriate.
Before prescribing, performs an allergy check (critical): asks about medication allergies and documents/acts on any reported allergy; failure is a critical safety error.
Before prescribing, considers drug interactions (noncritical): reviews current medications/OTC/herbals and flags major interactions relevant to the chosen antiseizure medication.

Before prescribing, considers contraindications (noncritical): screens for key factors affecting antiseizure choice/dosing (e.g., hepatic disease for valproate, pregnancy potential in adolescents, renal impairment, history of pancreatitis, thrombocytopenia).
Avoids implying there is a proven curative or disease-modifying therapy; does not recommend unproven treatments as standard of care.

Provides anticipatory guidance and safety-netting for seizures (when to seek emergency care, rescue plan if applicable) and discusses monitoring for adverse effects of the selected antiseizure medication.

steps: State/confirm Sandhoff disease diagnosis and briefly explain what it is (genetic GM2 gangliosidosis).
